Unique Circumstances: First modern international Olympic Games, organised by the International Olympic Committee, majority of athletes were Greek, winners initially received silver medals, main venue at Panathenaic Stadium, Athens chosen due to being birthplace of Ancient Olympic Games
Notable Performances: United States (most gold medals), Greece (most total medals), Spyridon Louis (marathon victory), Carl Schuhmann (most successful competitor)

Unique Circumstances: First Olympics in a non-leap year, part of the 1900 World's Fair, first time women participated, controversy over Sunday competitions, winners received cups or trophies instead of medals
Notable Performances: Not specified
Notable Debuts: Women participants, sailor Hélène de Pourtalès

Unique Circumstances: First Olympics outside Europe, low international participation, introduction of three-medal format, some events combined with US national championships
Notable Performances: Not specified
Notable Debuts: Boxing, dumbbells, freestyle wrestling, decathlon

Selection Process: London selected over Rome, Berlin, and Milan in 1904
Notable Circumstances: Relocated from Rome due to Mount Vesuvius eruption, longest modern Olympics at 187 days, White City Stadium as venue, first Games with 400m track standard

Unique Circumstances: Last Olympics with solid gold medals, first Asian nation participation, introduction of art competitions, women's diving and swimming, decathlon, pentathlon, electric timing in athletics, exclusion of boxing, rejection of figure skating, 8-year gap due to World War I
Notable Performances: United States (most gold medals), Sweden (most total medals), Jim Thorpe (decathlon and pentathlon winner)
Notable Debuts: Japan
Selection Process: Stockholm was the only bid and selected in 1909

Unique Circumstances: Tribute to Belgian people after World War I, aftermath of Paris Peace Conference, sanctions against losing nations, absence of Soviet Union and Germany, Deutsche Kampfspiele in Germany, sailing events in Ostend and Amsterdam
Notable Performances: United States (most gold and overall medals)
Bidding Process: Antwerp selected as host city after cancellation of 1916 Berlin Games, bid made by Baron Édouard de Laveleye in 1912

Unique Circumstances: Second time hosted by Paris, first city to host Olympics twice, selection process with six bids, significant financial loss, 17 sports with 126 events, 17 sports venues used
Notable Performances: United States (most gold and overall medals)
Notable Debuts: China, Ecuador, Ireland, Lithuania, Uruguay, Philippines, Latvia, Poland
Bidding Process: Paris selected over Amsterdam, Barcelona, Los Angeles, Prague, Rome
Notable Boycotts: Germany not invited by the Organizing Committee
Medal Count Leaders: United States (most gold and overall medals)

Unique Circumstances: First Winter Olympics, held in conjunction with Summer Olympics, retroactively designated as first Winter Games, tradition of Winter Olympics in same year as Summer Olympics until 1992
Notable Performances: Charles Jewtraw (first gold medalist), Sonja Henie (youngest competitor), Gillis Grafström (first athlete to defend Summer Olympic title at Winter Olympics)
Notable Debuts: Not specified

Unique Circumstances: Amsterdam's previous bids, Los Angeles as the only other candidate, financial report, proposal history, cancellation of 1916 Games, support for Antwerp and Paris, Los Angeles' unsuccessful bids, selection process for 1932 Games
Notable Performances: United States (most gold and overall medals)
Medal Count Leaders: United States (most gold and overall medals)

Unique Circumstances: First stand-alone Winter Olympics, replaced Nordic Games, challenging weather conditions, controversial speed-skating event cancellation
Notable Performances: N/A
Notable Debuts: Argentina, Estonia, Germany, Japan, Lithuania, Luxembourg, Mexico, Netherlands, Romania

Selection Process: Single bid from Los Angeles, selected by default
Notable Circumstances: Held during the Great Depression, 37 participating nations, US President Hoover did not attend, reported profit of US$1,000,000, single bid selection process, 15 sports venues used

Unique Circumstances: First of four Winter Olympics in the United States, awarded to Lake Placid, construction of world's largest ski jump in Big Pines, demonstration sports included, reduced number of participating nations compared to 1928 Games
Notable Bids: Bid from California X Olympiad Association

Bidding Process: Berlin won bid over Barcelona at the 29th IOC Session in 1931
Notable Features: New 100,000-seat stadium, six gymnasiums, televised Games, radio broadcasts to 41 countries, film by Leni Riefenstahl, promotion of Nazi ideals, exclusion of Jewish athletes, ticket revenues and profit

Notable Debuts: Australia, Bulgaria, Greece, Liechtenstein, Spain, Turkey

Unique Circumstances: Austerity Games, no new venues built, athletes housed in existing accommodation, Germany and Japan not invited, Soviet Union chose not to send athletes, record number of nations represented
Notable Performances: Fanny Blankers-Koen (four gold medals)

Unique Circumstances: First Winter Games after WWII, political atmosphere, challenges due to post-war conditions, first Winter Games under Sigfrid Edström's IOC presidency
Notable Performances: Dick Button, Barbara Ann Scott, Henri Oreiller
Notable Debuts: Haiti, Saudi Arabia

Unique Circumstances: Last of two consecutive Olympics in Northern Europe, most world records broken until 2008 Beijing Games, Helsinki originally selected for 1940 Olympics, cancelled due to WWII, Tokyo hosted 1964 Games, northernmost city to host Summer Olympics
Notable Performances: United States (most gold and overall medals)
Notable Debuts: Soviet Union, People's Republic of China, Hong Kong, Indonesia, Israel, Thailand, Saarland

Unique Circumstances: First modern athlete's village, first Winter Olympics in Northern Europe, returns of Japan and Germany, debut of Portugal and New Zealand, women in cross-country skiing
Notable Performances: Hjalmar Andersen (most decorated athlete), Germany (bobsleigh wins)
Notable Debuts: Japan, Germany, Portugal, New Zealand

Unique Circumstances: First Olympics in Southern Hemisphere, split location with equestrian events in Stockholm, held in November and December, tradition of national teams parading as one during closing ceremony
Notable Boycotts: Eight teams boycotted, four due to the Suez Crisis

Unique Circumstances: Venues within walking distance, reliance on corporate sponsorship, lack of snow at alpine skiing events, first Winter Olympics televised to a multi-national audience
Notable Performances: Toni Sailer (swept all three alpine skiing events)
Soviet Union (most gold and overall medals)
Notable Debuts: Morocco, San Marino, Sudan, Tunisia, Barbados, Jamaica, Trinidad and Tobago, Rhodesia, East Germany, West Germany
Bidding Process: Rome selected over Brussels, Mexico City, Tokyo, Detroit, Budapest, and Lausanne

Unique Circumstances: Intimate layout, venues built between 1956-1960, bobsled not included, Squaw Valley as an undeveloped resort, minimal facilities, surprise selection as host
Notable Debuts: Biathlon, women's speed skating
Selection Process: Inspired bid by Wayne Poulsen and Alexander Cushing, financial support from California, USOC approval, US Congress resolution supporting bid

Unique Circumstances: South Africa excluded due to apartheid, first Olympics in Asia, first color telecasts, telecast internationally without tapes, chronicled in Tokyo Olympiad film
Notable Performances: Not specified
Notable Circumstances: First Olympics in Asia, South Africa excluded due to apartheid, first international telecast without tapes, first color telecasts, chronicled in Tokyo Olympiad film

Unique Circumstances: Record number of athletes, luge debut, Soviet Union dominance, Austrian mobilization for snow, second Winter Games in Innsbruck in 1976
Notable Performances: Soviet Union (most gold and overall medals), Lidia Skoblikova, Klavdiya Boyarskikh, Sixten Jernberg, Christine and Marielle Goitschel
Notable Debuts: Luge, North Korea, India, Mongolia

Unique Circumstances: First Olympics in Latin America, first in Spanish-speaking country, all-weather track debut, electronic timekeeping debut, correlation to Mexican Student Movement, US medal dominance, South Africa's provisional invitation
Notable Performances: United States (most gold and overall medals)
Selection Process: Mexico City chosen over Detroit, Buenos Aires, and Lyon in 1963
Torch Relay Details: Recreated Christopher Columbus route, forged by James Metcalf
South Africa: Provisionally invited with conditions for integration by 1972

Unique Circumstances: First time East and West Germany entered separately, first IOC-ordered drug and gender testing, popularized Winter Olympics in the United States
Notable Performances: Jean-Claude Killy (3 gold medals), Peggy Fleming (1 gold medal)
Notable Debuts: East and West Germany competing separately
Selection Process: Application presented in November 1960, officially given to the IOC in February 1963

Unique Circumstances: Munich massacre, democratic representation of Germany, official motto "Die Heiteren Spiele", blue solar logo, sky-blue dirndls for hostesses, dachshund mascot "Waldi", Olympic Fanfare by Herbert Rehbein, Olympic Park, competition sites by Günther Behnisch, revolutionary stadium design
Notable Performances: Soviet Union (most gold and overall medals)
Bidding Process: Munich selected over Detroit, Madrid, and Montréal
Medal Count Leaders: Soviet Union (most gold and overall medals)
Notable Events: Munich massacre, second Summer Olympics in Germany
Logo: Blue solar logo by Otl Aicher
Olympic Park: Based on Frei Otto's plans, became Munich landmark
Competition Sites: Olympic swimming hall, Olympics Hall, Olympic Stadium
Design of Stadium: Revolutionary design with acrylic glass canopies

Unique Circumstances: First Winter Olympics outside Europe and North America, Sapporo initially awarded 1940 Games, later cancelled, significant government investment in infrastructure, successful trial run and international sport week, profitable broadcast rights
Notable Performances: Not specified
Notable Debuts: Republic of China, Philippines
Selection Process: Competed with Banff, Lahti, and Salt Lake City, awarded at 64th IOC Session in Rome in 1966

Unique Circumstances: First and only Summer Olympics in Canada, Toronto hosted the 1976 Summer Paralympics, Calgary and Vancouver later hosted Winter Olympics, political boycotts, New Zealand controversy
Notable Performances: Soviet Union (most gold and overall medals)
Bidding Process: Montreal selected over Moscow and Los Angeles
Notable Boycotts: 29 countries boycotted due to New Zealand controversy

Selection Process: Denver selected but withdrew, Whistler declined, Salt Lake City offer rejected, Innsbruck selected in 1973
Notable Circumstances: Games awarded to Innsbruck after Denver's withdrawal, second time Innsbruck hosted Winter Olympics, bids from Denver, Sion, Tampere, and Vancouver, Denver's rejection led to Innsbruck selection
Mascot: Schneemann (snowman in a red Tyrolean hat)

Unique Circumstances: First Olympics in an Eastern Bloc country, only Summer Games in a Slavic language-speaking country, final Olympics under Michael Morris' IOC Presidency, significant boycotts due to Soviet-Afghan War
Notable Performances: Soviet Union (most gold and overall medals), United States led boycott with 66 countries
Bidding Process: Moscow selected over Los Angeles at the 75th IOC Session in Vienna in 1974
Notable Boycotts: 66 countries boycotted due to Soviet-Afghan War

Unique Circumstances: Second time hosting, venues from 1932 Games renovated, criticized organization, last Winter Games in a city <15,000 inhabitants
Notable Performances: Eric Heiden (most medals), American ice hockey team (Miracle on Ice)
Notable Debuts: People's Republic of China, Cyprus, Costa Rica
Selection Process: Lake Placid elected at 75th IOC Session in Vienna in 1974
Significant Events: Eric Heiden's record, Miracle on Ice victory

Unique Circumstances: Second time hosted by Los Angeles, first Summer Olympics under Juan Antonio Samaranch's IOC presidency, financially successful, record number of participating nations, significant boycotts
Notable Performances: United States (most gold and overall medals), Romania, West Germany
Notable Boycotts: Fourteen Eastern Bloc countries, Albania, Iran, Libya

Unique Circumstances: First Winter Olympics in a Slavic language-speaking country, only Winter Olympics in a communist country before 2022, second consecutive Olympics in a communist country, held in Sarajevo and nearby resorts, disrupted by extreme weather conditions
Notable Performances: Marja-Liisa Hämäläinen (Finland), Jure Franko (Yugoslavia), East Germany (dominant in women's speed skating and bobsleigh)
Notable Debuts: Egypt, British Virgin Islands, Monaco, Puerto Rico, Senegal

Unique Circumstances: Second summer Olympics in Asia, first in South Korea, first in a developing country, last Olympics of the Cold War, significant media coverage
Notable Performances: Soviet Union (55 gold, 132 total medals), South Korea (12 gold, 33 total medals)
Notable Boycotts: North Korea, Cuba, Albania, Ethiopia, Seychelles, Nicaragua, Madagascar

Unique Circumstances: 16-day duration, majority of events in Calgary, skiing events at Nakiska and Canmore, last attended Olympics for Soviet Union and East Germany, record number of participating nations
Notable Performances: Matti Nykänen (3 gold medals), Yvonne van Gennip (3 gold medals)
Notable Debuts: Jamaica national bobsleigh team

Unique Circumstances: First Summer Olympics in Spain, end of Cold War, first unaffected by boycotts since 1972, South Africa re-invited after 32-year ban, last two consecutive Olympics in Western Europe, Barcelona selected over Amsterdam, Belgrade, Birmingham, Brisbane, and Paris
Notable Performances: Unified Team (most gold and overall medals)
Selection Process: Barcelona selected during the 91st IOC Session in Lausanne, Switzerland, won majority vote with 47 out of 85 votes

Unique Circumstances: Last Winter Olympics held in the same year as Summer Olympics, third Winter Olympics in France, first of two consecutive Olympic games in Western Europe, events held in multiple villages, debut of Unified Team and Germany, last to include demonstration sports and outdoor speed skating rink
Notable Performances: N/A
Notable Debuts: Eight nations
Selection Process: Won bid over Sofia, Falun, Lillehammer, Cortina d'Ampezzo, Anchorage, and Berchtesgaden in 1986

Unique Circumstances: Only Winter Olympics two years after the previous edition, first Winter Games in a different year from Summer Olympics, second Winter Games in Norway, fourth Olympics in a Nordic country, northernmost city to host Olympics, last of three consecutive Olympics in Europe, events held in multiple locations
Notable Debuts: 14 countries, including nine former Soviet republics
Notable Aspects: Introduction of stricter qualifying rules, new events in short track speed skating and aerials, speed skating moved indoors, Olympic Truce in effect, almost two million spectators

Unique Circumstances: Centennial of the 1896 Summer Olympics, first Summer Olympics since 1924 held in a different year than Winter Olympics, part of new IOC practice, last Summer Olympics in North America until 2028, first of two consecutive Summer Olympics in predominantly English-speaking countries
Notable Performances: United States (most gold and overall medals)
Notable Debuts: Beach volleyball, mountain biking, softball, lightweight rowing, women's fencing, team rhythmic gymnastics, women's association football

Unique Circumstances: Record number of athletes and participating nations, introduction of new sports, first Winter Olympics under Juan Antonio Samaranch's IOC presidency, professional NHL players allowed in men's ice hockey, debuts of several countries
Notable Performances: Germany (most medals), Norway, Russia, Canada, Larisa Lazutina
Notable Debuts: Curling, snowboarding, women's ice hockey, Azerbaijan, Kenya, Macedonia, Uruguay, Venezuela
Subsequent Hosts: 1998 Winter Paralympics, final Winter Olympics under Juan Antonio Samaranch's IOC presidency
Selection Process: Selected over Salt Lake City, Östersund, Jaca, and Aosta in 1991

Unique Circumstances: Second time hosting Olympics in Australia, first in Southern Hemisphere, cost estimated at A$6.6 billion, final Olympics under Juan Antonio Samaranch's IOC presidency, last of two consecutive Summer Olympics in English-speaking country, Brisbane to host 2032 Summer Olympics
Notable Performances: Not specified
Selection Process: Sydney selected as host city in 1993

Unique Circumstances: First Olympics with combined organizing committee, budgetary surplus, venues used post-Olympics, United States Olympic Committee support for future bids
Notable Performances: Norway (most gold medals), Germany (most total medals), Australia (first Southern Hemisphere gold medals)
Selection Process: Chosen over Québec City, Sion, and Östersund in 1995

Unique Circumstances: First time since 1996 all NOCs present, Athens' second hosting, new medal design, return to birthplace, improved infrastructure, potential impact on Greek debt crisis
Notable Features: All countries with NOCs in attendance, return to birthplace, new medal design, consecutive Southern European Olympics, impact on Athens infrastructure, success of the games
Consecutive Southern European Olympics: Followed by 2006 Winter Olympics in Turin, Italy
Impact on Infrastructure: Improved infrastructure including new airport, ring road, subway system
Success of the Games: Deemed a success with rising competition standards

Selection Process: Turin chosen as host city at the 109th IOC Session in Seoul, South Korea in June 1999, Selection College chose Turin and Sion as finalist cities, full IOC Session voted on finalist cities
Logo Description: Stylized profile of Mole Antonelliana in white and blue ice crystals, symbolizing snow, sky, new technologies, and Olympic spirit of community
Notable Details: Second time Italy hosted Winter Olympics, first being in 1956 in Cortina d'Ampezzo, Italy also hosted 1960 Summer Olympics in Rome, Turin selected as host city in June 1999, controversies surrounding 1998 and 2002 Winter Olympics votes, new election procedures adopted by IOC, special Selection College chosen to pick finalist cities, six cities launched candidacies, Sion and Turin chosen as finalists, full IOC Session voted on finalist cities

Unique Circumstances: First time hosted by China, third Summer Olympics in East Asia, second Summer Olympics in a communist state, heavy government investment in facilities and transport systems
Selection Process: Beijing awarded the Games over four competitors in 2001
Venues Used: 37 venues, including 12 constructed for the Games
Torch Relay Distance: Longest distance for an Olympic Torch relay

Unique Circumstances: Third Olympics hosted by Canada, first in British Columbia, first gold in official sport as host nation, record gold medal count, flag handover in 2006
Notable Performances: Canada (record gold medals), first host nation to lead gold medal count since 1952

Unique Circumstances: London selected as host city for the third time, extensive redevelopment and sustainability focus, use of existing and new venues, inclusion of women's boxing and female competitors from all eligible countries
Notable Performances: United States (most gold and overall medals), China (second most total medals), Great Britain (third most total medals), Michael Phelps (most decorated athlete)
Notable Debuts: Saudi Arabia, Qatar, Brunei (first female athletes)
Bidding Process: London selected over Moscow, New York City, Madrid, and Paris in 2005
Medal Count Leaders: United States (most gold and overall medals)

Unique Circumstances: First Olympics under Thomas Bach's IOC presidency, held in a CIS state, record number of events, most expensive Games in history, broadcast audience of 2.1 billion people
Notable Debuts: Biathlon mixed relay, women's ski jumping, mixed-team figure skating, mixed-team luge, half-pipe skiing, ski and snowboard slopestyle, snowboard parallel slalom
Selection Process: Sochi selected as host city in July 2007

Unique Circumstances: First Olympics in South America, first in a Portuguese-speaking country, first summer edition in host country's winter, first in Latin America since 1968, first in Southern Hemisphere since 2000, held under Thomas Bach's IOC presidency
Notable Debuts: Kosovo, South Sudan, Refugee Olympic Team
Medal Count Leaders: United States (most gold and overall medals)

Unique Circumstances: Second time South Korea hosted Olympics, first Winter Olympics in South Korea, record number of events, first Winter Olympics in mainland Asia, part of three consecutive Olympic Games in East Asia, debut of four new events, Russian doping scandal
Notable Performances: N/A
Notable Debuts: Ecuador, Eritrea, Kosovo, Malaysia, Nigeria, Singapore

Unique Circumstances: Second time hosting Olympics, record number of events, debut of big air freestyle skiing and women's monobob, concerns over human rights violations, COVID-19 protocols, Haiti and Saudi Arabia debut, Norway tops medal table, China's most successful Winter Olympics
Notable Debuts: Haiti, Saudi Arabia

Unique Circumstances: Centenary of the Paris Games of 1924, sixth Olympic Games hosted by France, second city to host Summer Olympics three times, first of two consecutive Olympic games in Europe, followed by 2026 Winter Olympics in Italy
Bidding Process: Proposal to elect 2024 and 2028 Olympic host cities concurrently approved in 2017, Paris selected to host 2024 Games

Unique Circumstances: Fourth Olympic Games hosted in Italy, first featuring multiple host cities, first Winter Olympics with opening and closing ceremonies in different locations, 20th anniversary of 2006 Winter Olympics in Turin, 70th anniversary of 1956 Winter Olympics in Cortina d'Ampezzo, last of two consecutive Olympics in Europe
Selection Process: Milan and Cortina d'Ampezzo elected over Stockholm–Åre bid at 134th IOC Session in Lausanne in 2019
Host Cities: Milan, Lombardy and Cortina d'Ampezzo, Veneto
Notable Additions: Proposal for ski mountaineering approved for inclusion

Unique Circumstances: First Summer Games in the US since 1996, first Olympics in LA since 1984, joint award with 2024 Games, record-breaking use of existing facilities, third three-time host city, fifth Summer Games in the US
Bidding Process: Jointly awarded with 2024 Games, Los Angeles bid with additional funding, Paris confirmed as 2024 host

Unique Circumstances: Third Summer Games in Australia, fourth in Southern Hemisphere, second in host city's winter, Brisbane selected through new bid process, second consecutive Summer Games in English-speaking country
Subsequent Hosts: 2028 Summer Olympics in Los Angeles, United States
Bidding Process: Brisbane selected as winning bid on 21 July 2021, first host city chosen through new bid process
